title: Caruana vs Carlsen — 2018 WCC, Game 3
description: A quiet Rossolimo Sicilian where Carlsen's rare 6...Qc7 and patient maneuvering earn only vague chances before Caruana defends accurately to a draw. Annotated by GM Sam Shankland.

conceptChecks.prompt: Carlsen plays the rare 6...Qc7 ("!?") instead of the usual 6...Nf6. What is the annotator's view of it?

options.text: A very rare move aimed at getting Caruana out of his book and comfort zone, not necessarily objectively better than 6...Nf6.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Magnus chose a very rare move. I am not at all convinced that it is superior to Nf6... but it keeps in line with his willingness to mix things up a bit early on to try to get Caruana out of his book and comfort zone."

options.text: The objectively best and most common move in the position.
options.explanation: The opposite — the annotator says 6...Nf6 is "far and away the most common choice" and is unconvinced 6...Qc7 is superior.

options.text: A move that wins a pawn in the opening.
options.explanation: No such claim; the point is to surprise Caruana, not to grab material.

conceptChecks.hint: Why would Magnus pick a rare move so early?

conceptChecks.prompt: What does the annotator say about 10...Bg4 ("!?")?

options.text: It is a direct move that simplifies a lot into a position Magnus was sure he would not lose, even if not objectively best.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "This is a very direct move. It's probably not best in a higher chess sense, but it simplifies the position a lot, to one that Magnus was probably quite sure he would not lose."

options.text: It is the engine's top choice and clearly best.
options.explanation: The annotator says the machine preferred 10...cxb4 and that Bg4 is "probably not best in a higher chess sense."

options.text: It wins material with a tactic on f3.
options.explanation: No tactic is claimed; the idea is simplification toward a safe draw.

conceptChecks.hint: Is the annotator praising accuracy or practicality?

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does 12...cxb4 ("!") work here, even though the annotator says "normally this move is wrong"?

options.text: Because Black can immediately follow with ...a5, trading pawns and neutralizing the otherwise problematic open c-file.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Normally this move is wrong, but the position is different because Black can immediately get the a7-a5 advance in. This trades off some pawns and neutralizes the potentially problematic open c-file."

options.text: Because it simply wins the b4-pawn for nothing.
options.explanation: It is a structural trade enabled by ...a5, not a free pawn win.

options.text: Because capturing on b4 is always correct in the Rossolimo.
options.explanation: The opposite — the annotator says it is "normally" wrong and works only due to this position's specifics.

conceptChecks.hint: What move does Black get to play right after capturing?

conceptChecks.prompt: What is the strategic idea behind 29...Ng5 ("!")?

options.text: To provoke White's pawn to h4, fixing it on a dark square where it may become a long-term weakness.
options.explanation: Correct. The annotator explains that "pawns do not move backward" and that 29...Ng5 forces "the pawn to h4, where it will be worse placed" and "fixed on a dark square."
The pawn is not won; it is merely provoked forward to a worse square.

options.text: To set up a direct mating attack on White's king.
options.explanation: No attack is mentioned; this is a slow positional provocation.

conceptChecks.hint: The annotator even plugs his book on this exact theme.

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator gives 37...fxe4 a "?!". What was the problem with it?

options.text: It releases the tension and makes the position "absolutely drawn"; 37...f4 was a better winning try.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "After this it's absolutely drawn," with the sideline 37...f4 given as "a better try."
The annotator calls it drawn, not losing.

options.text: It was the only move available.
options.explanation: No — 37...f4 was an alternative the annotator preferred.

conceptChecks.hint: What happened to Black's winning chances after this capture?

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does the annotator call 41.Ke2 ("!") "the last important move"?

options.text: It sidesteps the losing 41.f3?, which would walk into zugzwang; Ke2 holds easily.
options.explanation: Correct. The note praises Ke2 and shows that "41.f3? Would weaken White's position too much, and indeed he loses" after 41...Bb6! due to zugzwang.

options.text: It wins the game for White.
options.explanation: It only secures the draw; the position is equal.

options.text: It launches a mating attack against Black's king.
options.explanation: There is no attack; it is a quiet, accurate holding move.

conceptChecks.hint: What move would have lost, and why?
